directions.name: Reduce stage-recycle barriers in the peeled 64x384 hot loop
directions.hypothesis: The current best base confirms the right family: peeled fixed-shape hot kernel plus trimmed export, with deeper single-skew overlap improving again to 35.677088 ms. The residual tradeoff is now clear in the hot kernel profile: short scoreboard collapsed to 0.39% and DRAM dropped to 27.32%, but barrier stall climbed to 15.46% while tensor active slipped slightly versus the previous base. That makes the best next move a bounded refinement of the peeled overlap schedule itself: keep the same single-skew feed path and 64x384 tile, but reduce the number or scope of full-CTA stage-recycle barriers so the overlap gain survives without paying as much synchronization cost.
directions.expected_bottleneck: CTA-wide synchronization and stage handoff overhead inside the peeled 64x384 steady-state loop.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:567-607 (peeled hot-loop prefill, future-tile refill, wait_group_1, and full-CTA barriers)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:538-542 (two-stage shared buffers whose reuse currently forces the recycle synchronization)
directions.risk: The current kernel is already the best custom result, so reducing barriers can easily re-expose short-scoreboard or create stage-reuse races if the synchronization boundary moves too far.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if barrier stall does not fall on top of the current base, or if reducing synchronization causes short-scoreboard or runtime to regress.

directions.name: Trim the peeled hot-kernel export path beyond the current quad writeback
directions.hypothesis: The winning family already improved the export path by switching the peeled hot kernel to quad BF16 writeback, but the kernel still routes every accumulator tile through shared memory before global stores. With LSU requests still around 80.65% and data-pipe LSU wavefronts around 50.35%, the next complementary move is to make the peeled export path cheaper again without touching the feed family that previously regressed. That could mean fewer shared round-trips, less warp synchronization around writeback, or a more direct packed-store mapping on top of the current peeled kernel.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Epilogue-side shared export and LSU pressure after the peeled hot loop finishes MMA.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:201-214 (current packed BF16 pair and quad store helpers)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:629-659 (peeled hot-kernel shared export, quad packing, and writeback loop)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:41-58 (shared-memory budget and c_shared stage count that constrain export choices)
directions.risk: The export path is correctness-sensitive and already partially optimized, so further changes may save little or break layout if the lane-to-output mapping shifts incorrectly.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if LSU and shared-write pressure do not improve or if export-path changes fail to produce a measurable end-to-end gain.

directions.name: Peel and specialize the fixed 64x96 tail kernel to match the hot path
directions.hypothesis: The 64x384 hot path is now fixed-shape peeled, but the final 96-column tail still runs through the generic tensor-core kernel and generic launch flow. That tail is only one CTA column wide, so it is not the top-upside item, but it is the clearest remaining part of the fixed benchmark pipeline that still ignores the same fixed-shape advantages that helped the hot kernel win. The tail profile still shows high barrier and short-scoreboard stalls, so a small peeled fixed-K tail kernel is a coherent lower-ranked follow-up that stays aligned with the winning specialization family and avoids reopening the broadly failed B-feed and live-set rewrite families.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual tail overhead from using the generic 64x96 tensor-core kernel on a fixed-shape tail region.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:409-526 (current generic tensor-core kernel that still serves the 64x96 tail)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:528-666 (peeled fixed-shape hot kernel that can be mirrored in smaller fixed-tail form)
src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:689-722 (fixed-shape dispatch that currently launches the generic TensorCoreTile96 tail)
directions.risk: The tail covers only 96 of 7776 columns, so the payoff may be below noise even if the specialized tail is cleaner in isolation.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the specialized tail does not produce a measurable full-run gain or if the tail-only improvement is drowned out by variance.

notes: Round 2/5 diagnosis prepared from the current best custom run 20260419_102608_bf16_gemm_v1_2872f92 at 35.677088 ms. Recent history is applied explicitly: broad B-feed rewrites and phased live-set rewrites regressed, peeled hot-kernel specialization plus trimmed export won, and deeper single-skew overlap improved again. Ranking therefore keeps the peeled 64x384 hot kernel as the base, prioritizes barrier/stage-recycle cleanup next, then further export trimming, and leaves tail specialization as the lower-upside third option.
